refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints from stakeholders), the system produces a traceable set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy those inputs. Verify traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall capture worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from captured worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to source mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a mission-context capture module that accepts worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) and applies a deterministic mapping function to derive performance targets; maintain a versioned baseline registry with change history and traceability links.

DP-B: Establish a parameterized performance-target template library indexed by worksite class/mission type; populate templates via structured intake form that references safety plan and operational policies; log all instantiations and updates to a persistent audit trail.

DP-C: Deploy a rule-based inference engine that ingests worksite mission context (safety constraints, operational modes, hazard profiles) and applies domain-specific rules to synthesize performance requirements; store derived targets and rule-execution traces in a queryable repository.

DP-D: Create a manual performance-target specification process where domain experts review worksite inputs and document targets in a standardized template; implement a change-control board to approve updates and maintain a calibration history log.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Establish a parameterized performance-target template library indexed by worksite class/mission type; populate templates via structured intake form that references safety plan and operational policies; log all instantiations and updates to a persistent audit trail.

potential_sources: Template library design must remain decoupled from specific worksite implementations to avoid tight coupling between mission context and target derivation logic.
Intake form structure should not embed domain-specific rules; it should remain a neutral data-collection interface to preserve independence from downstream performance-validation modules.
Audit trail implementation must not assume a particular persistence technology; use abstraction to allow swapping storage backends without affecting the derivation logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B balances automation (template-driven) with flexibility (parameterization) and maintains clear traceability (audit trail). It avoids over-coupling by keeping the template library separate from intake logic and the audit trail separate from derivation. DP-A risks tight coupling between mapping function and worksite inputs; DP-C introduces inference complexity that may obscure traceability; DP-D lacks scalability and automation, increasing manual error risk.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission contexts can be meaningfully classified into a finite set of types suitable for templating. Risk: If worksite diversity is high, template coverage may be incomplete, requiring frequent custom extensions.

Assumption: Safety plan and operational policies are available in machine-readable or easily parseable form. Risk: If inputs are unstructured documents, intake form design becomes brittle; recommend constraint that inputs must conform to a defined schema.

Assumption: Performance targets derived from templates are sufficient to meet acceptance criteria without additional expert review. Risk: Mitigation required: ensure intake form includes a review/approval gate before targets are finalized.

Assumption: Audit trail granularity (what is logged) is sufficient for traceability. Risk: If logging is too coarse, root-cause analysis of target changes becomes difficult; recommend logging both the input state and the template rule applied.

Assumption: Worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies) remain stable during a performance-target baseline period. Risk: If inputs change frequently, baseline validity degrades; recommend explicit versioning and re-derivation triggers.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR-CR-NGHE-005 to explicitly state: (1) Worksite mission context inputs shall be provided in a defined schema (e.g., structured form or data model) to enable deterministic template matching; (2) Performance targets shall be derived via template instantiation and logged with full traceability to input version and template rule; (3) A change-control process shall govern updates to baselines, with approval gates and audit-trail retention for at least [X] years. This revision clarifies the boundary between intake (IM responsibility) and derivation (DP responsibility) and ensures DP-B can be realized without ambiguity.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized management of performance-target templates, parameterized by worksite class and mission type; structured intake form processing; audit trail persistence; template instantiation and lifecycle tracking.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission/worksite class context to select and instantiate appropriate performance-target template.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety constraint violations or template-driven safety thresholds when performance targets are breached.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational data to validate against instantiated performance targets and log audit trail events.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services owns safety policies, operational constraints, and audit compliance. Template library, intake form logic, and audit trail are inherently safety-domain concerns. Minor diffusion: Tasking interface required to receive mission context; Telemetry required to validate targets. No algorithmic or data duplication needed outside this boundary.

Moderate fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator and would require Safety Services to be a sub-component. Template library and audit trail would be logically correct but architecturally diffuse—safety-specific logic would be embedded in a general solution layer, violating separation of concerns. Intake form processing would require bidirectional coupling to multiple subsystems.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational asset, not a policy or governance layer. Performance-target templates are mission and safety-policy abstractions, not vehicle-specific hardware concerns. Audit trail and intake form belong in a governance/safety domain, not vehicle control. High diffusion: Safety logic would leak into vehicle module; template management would require external coordination.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/communication subsystem, not a policy or template management layer. Performance targets are safety/mission governance concerns, not teleoperation-specific. Audit trail and intake form are orthogonal to remote operation mechanics. High diffusion: Safety policy logic would contaminate teleoperation module; template instantiation would require external orchestration.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of performance-target templates because: (1) templates encode safety policies and operational constraints; (2) structured intake forms reference safety plans and operational policies (explicitly stated in DP); (3) audit trail is a compliance and governance artifact; (4) template instantiation is driven by mission/worksite class, which Safety Services must validate against safety constraints. Coupling to Tasking (mission context) and Telemetry (validation) is minimal and unidirectional.
containment_risks: Risk: Intake form may require domain-specific fields from Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems (e.g., vehicle payload limits, teleoperation bandwidth constraints). Mitigation: Define intake form as a schema that accepts structured metadata from external modules via Tasking interface; Safety Services does not need to know implementation details.

Risk: Audit trail persistence may require database or logging infrastructure not owned by Safety Services. Mitigation: Treat audit trail as a logical responsibility of Safety Services; delegate persistence to a shared infrastructure layer (e.g., logging service) via a well-defined interface.

Risk: Template updates may be triggered by operational feedback from Vehicle or Teleoperation. Mitigation: Updates flow through SafetyAlert or Tasking interfaces; Safety Services remains the authoritative owner of template state.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B correctly addresses FR-CR-NGHE-005 by establishing a parameterized template library indexed by worksite class/mission type, structured intake form processing, and persistent audit trail. The DP explicitly references safety plan and operational policies as inputs, directly satisfying the requirement to derive performance requirements from worksite mission context. The three components (template library, intake form, audit trail) are logically sufficient and necessary to fulfill the functional requirement.

diffusion_risks: Intake form schema definition: Risk that domain-specific fields from Vehicle (payload limits) or Teleoperation (bandwidth constraints) leak into Safety Services ownership. Mitigation: Formalize intake form as a schema contract; external modules provide structured metadata via Tasking interface without Safety Services needing implementation knowledge.

Audit trail persistence infrastructure: Risk that Safety Services becomes responsible for database/logging implementation details. Mitigation: Delegate persistence to a shared infrastructure layer (logging service) with a well-defined append/query interface; Safety Services owns the logical audit trail semantics, not the storage mechanism.

Template update triggers from operational feedback: Risk that Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems directly modify templates, bypassing Safety Services governance. Mitigation: Enforce that all updates flow through SafetyAlert or Tasking interfaces; Safety Services remains the authoritative owner of template state and change control.

Telemetry validation coupling: Risk that Telemetry interface becomes bidirectional or creates tight coupling. Mitigation: Telemetry provides read-only operational data to Safety Services for validation; Safety Services does not push configuration back to Telemetry.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the correct IM owner with a containment score of 0.92 and diffusion score of 0.08. The module naturally owns safety policies, operational constraints, and audit compliance. Template library, intake form logic, and audit trail are inherently safety-domain concerns. External interfaces (Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Telemetry in) are minimal and unidirectional, indicating strong cohesion. No algorithmic or data duplication is required outside this boundary. The alternative candidates (NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation) all exhibit high diffusion (0.35–0.85) due to violation of separation of concerns and contamination of non-safety modules with policy logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require: (1) documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs—satisfied by structured intake form and template instantiation logging; (2) history of baselines/calibration updates—satisfied by persistent audit trail. Both are directly observable and auditable outputs of Safety Services. Traceability can be verified by querying audit trail for each instantiation event and cross-referencing with worksite safety plan and operational policies provided via Tasking interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services is allocated ownership of performance-target template management, intake form processing, and audit trail persistence. Tasking provides mission/worksite class context as input; Telemetry provides operational data for validation; SafetyAlert emits constraint violations. The allocation respects domain boundaries: safety governance remains in Safety Services; mission context remains in Tasking; operational telemetry remains in Telemetry. No ambiguity in responsibility or interface ownership.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the intake form schema as a published contract (e.g., JSON Schema or OpenAPI specification) that Safety Services accepts from Tasking. This decouples Safety Services from knowledge of how external modules populate the schema.

Explicitly document the audit trail interface: define what events are logged (template creation, instantiation, update, deletion), the immutability guarantee, and the query API. Treat audit trail as a logical responsibility of Safety Services; delegate persistence to a shared logging infrastructure layer.

Add a constraint to DP-B: 'All template updates must be authorized and logged; no direct modification by external modules.' This reinforces Safety Services as the authoritative owner of template state.

Define the Telemetry validation interface: specify which performance targets are validated against which telemetry signals, and how validation failures trigger SafetyAlert events. This clarifies the read-only nature of the Telemetry interface.

Consider adding a versioning mechanism to the template library: track template versions, baseline calibration dates, and policy change history. This strengthens traceability to worksite safety plan and operational policies as required by acceptance criteria.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the DP-IM gate identifies four concrete diffusion risks with specific mitigation strategies that require architectural elaboration at the next decomposition level. Given the high safety criticality of this requirement (FR-CR-NGHE-005), deriving sub-FRs to explicitly address intake form schema governance, audit trail persistence contracts, template update authorization, and telemetry validation coupling is necessary to ensure these mitigations are architecturally realized and not left as implementation assumptions. The recommended revisions are not defects in the current DP but rather signals that the design is ready for deeper decomposition to formalize interfaces and ownership boundaries.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-CR-NGHE-005-A: Accept and validate structured intake form data from external Tasking module without requiring Safety Services to know the schema population logic.
FR-CR-NGHE-005-B: Persist and query audit trail events (template creation, instantiation, update, deletion) with immutability guarantees, delegating storage mechanism to shared infrastructure.

FR-CR-NGHE-005-C: Enforce that all template library updates are authorized, logged, and traceable to worksite safety plan or operational policy changes; prevent direct modification by external modules.
FR-CR-NGHE-005-D: Validate performance targets against operational telemetry signals via read-only Telemetry interface; trigger SafetyAlert on constraint violations without bidirectional coupling.

revise.why: Incorporate the four recommended revisions into DP-B to formalize the intake form schema contract, audit trail interface semantics, template update authorization constraint, and telemetry validation coupling rules. These revisions strengthen the DP by making implicit assumptions explicit and reducing ambiguity for downstream implementation. The revisions do not change the core logic of DP-B but clarify its architectural boundaries and interface contracts.

assumptions: Tasking module will provide intake form data conforming to a published schema contract; Safety Services does not need to understand how external modules populate this schema.
A shared logging/persistence infrastructure layer exists or will be provided to handle audit trail storage; Safety Services owns the logical semantics of what is logged, not the storage mechanism.
Template library updates are infrequent and can be gated through a formal authorization process; no real-time dynamic template generation is required.
Telemetry interface is unidirectional (read-only from Safety Services perspective); no configuration feedback from Safety Services to Telemetry is required.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are provided as structured inputs via Tasking interface at mission start; Safety Services does not retrieve or interpret free-form policy documents.
open_risks: Intake form schema evolution: If Tasking or other external modules change the schema without coordinating with Safety Services, template instantiation may fail or produce incomplete performance targets. Mitigation: Establish schema versioning and deprecation policy; Safety Services must validate schema conformance at intake time.

Audit trail query performance: If audit trail grows large (many worksites, frequent instantiations), query latency may impact real-time validation. Mitigation: Define indexing strategy (by worksite class, mission type, timestamp) and acceptable query latency SLAs.

Template authorization bottleneck: If all template updates require manual approval, operational agility may be constrained. Mitigation: Define approval workflows (e.g., automated approval for policy-driven updates, manual approval for safety-critical changes).

Telemetry signal availability: If required telemetry signals are not available or delayed, validation cannot occur. Mitigation: Define fallback behavior (e.g., conservative constraints, alerts to operator) when telemetry is unavailable.

Cross-module traceability: Audit trail must link back to worksite safety plan and operational policies; if these are not formally versioned or stored, traceability is lost. Mitigation: Require Tasking to provide policy version identifiers and safety plan references as part of intake form.
evidence_needed: Formalized intake form schema (JSON Schema or OpenAPI specification) with examples for different worksite classes and mission types.
Audit trail interface specification: event types, immutability guarantees, query API, retention policy, and indexing strategy.
Template update authorization policy: approval workflows, roles, and audit logging for all state changes.
Telemetry validation mapping: which performance targets are validated against which telemetry signals, validation thresholds, and SafetyAlert trigger conditions.
Traceability matrix: linking each performance target to worksite safety plan requirements and operational policy constraints, with version control.
Integration test plan: demonstrating intake form processing, template instantiation, audit trail logging, and telemetry validation across representative worksite scenarios.
